title: Find Duplicate in Array
description: Given an integer array of size n containing numbers from 1 to n-1, where exactly one number appears twice, find and return the duplicate number. You must solve it without modifying the array and using only O(1) extra space.

examples.explanation: The number 2 appears twice in the array.

title: Rotate Array by K Steps
description: Given an integer array nums, rotate the array to the right by k steps in-place. The rotation should be done without using extra space for another array. Return the modified array.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) that has the largest product, and return that product. The array can contain negative numbers and zeros.

examples.explanation: Subarray [2, 3] has the largest product = 6.

title: Two Sum with HashMap
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to the target. Each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. Return indices in ascending order.

title: Group Anagrams Together
description: Given an array of strings, group all anagrams together and return a list of groups. The order of groups and the order within each group does not matter. Two strings are anagrams if one can be formed by rearranging the letters of the other.

examples.explanation: eat, tea, and ate are anagrams; tan and nat are anagrams; bat has no anagram.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
description: Given an unsorted array of integers, find the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. Your algorithm must run in O(n) time complexity. Consecutive elements are integers that differ by exactly 1.

examples.explanation: The longest consecutive sequence is [1, 2, 3, 4], with length 4.

title: Maximum Average Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, find the contiguous subarray of length exactly k that has the maximum average value. Return that maximum average value as a double.

examples.explanation: Subarray [12,-5,-6,50] has sum 51, average 51/4 = 12.75.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeating characters. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.

examples.explanation: The longest substring without repeating characters is "abc", with length 3.

title: Minimum Window Containing All Characters
description: Given two strings s and t, return the minimum length substring of s that contains all characters of t (including duplicates). If no such substring exists, return an empty string. If multiple valid substrings exist, return any one of minimum length.

examples.explanation: BANC is the smallest substring of s that contains A, B, and C.

title: Pair Sum Equals Target
description: Given a sorted array of integers and a target sum, determine if there exists a pair of elements in the array that sum up to the target. Return true if such a pair exists, otherwise return false. Each element may only be used once.

examples.explanation: 2 + 4 = 6, so a valid pair exists.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted integer array nums, remove duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears only once. Return the count k of unique elements. The first k elements of nums should hold the unique values in sorted order.

examples.explanation: After removal, the first 5 elements are [1,2,3,4,5] and k=5.

title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water can be trapped after raining. Use the two-pointer technique to solve it in O(n) time and O(1) space.

examples.explanation: The elevation map can trap 6 units of rain water in total.

title: Valid Parentheses Checker
description: Given a string containing only the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. A string is valid if every opening bracket is closed by the same type of bracket and in the correct order.

examples.explanation: Every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket in the correct order.

title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
description: Evaluate the value of an arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation (RPN). Valid operators are +, -, *, and /. Each operand may be an integer or another expression. Division truncates toward zero.

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
description: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram bar heights where the width of each bar is 1, find the area of the largest rectangle that can be formed in the histogram.

examples.explanation: The largest rectangle has area = 10 (bars at index 2 and 3 with height 5).

title: First Non-Repeating Character in Stream
description: Given a stream of characters, find the first non-repeating character at each point in the stream. For each character added, return the first character that has appeared exactly once so far, or '#' if no such character exists.

examples.explanation: After a: a. After aa: #. After aab: b. After aabb: #. After aabbc: c.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Given an array of integers nums and a sliding window of size k, return the maximum value in each window position as the window slides from left to right. There are n - k + 1 windows in total.

title: Task Scheduler with Cooldown
description: Given a list of tasks labeled with uppercase letters and a cooldown integer n, determine the minimum number of time units needed to finish all tasks. Identical tasks must be separated by at least n time units. You may insert idle time if needed.

examples.explanation: A -> B -> idle -> A -> B -> idle -> A -> B. Total 8 units.

title: Reverse a Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list in-place and return the new head. You must reverse the pointers iteratively without allocating any extra data structures.

title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
description: Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. A cycle occurs when a node's next pointer points back to a previous node. Return true if a cycle exists, false otherwise. Use O(1) extra memory.

examples.explanation: The tail node points back to node at index 1, creating a cycle.

title: Merge K Sorted Linked Lists
description: Given an array of k sorted linked lists, merge all the linked lists into one sorted linked list and return its head. The merged list should be sorted in non-decreasing order.

examples.explanation: Merging all three lists in sorted order gives [1,1,2,3,4,4,5,6].

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (mirror it) and return its root. Inverting means swapping the left and right children of every node in the tree.

examples.explanation: After inverting, the left subtree becomes the right subtree and vice versa at every level.

title: Level Order Traversal of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal as a list of lists. Each inner list should contain the values of nodes at that level from left to right.

examples.explanation: Level 0 has [3], level 1 has [9, 20], level 2 has [15, 7].

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize that string back to the original tree. The encoded format is your choice, but the deserialized tree must be structurally identical to the original.

examples.explanation: After serializing and deserializing, the tree structure must be preserved exactly.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given an m x n 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands. An island is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically. You may assume all four edges of the grid are surrounded by water.

examples.explanation: There are two islands: one at top-left (3 cells) and one at bottom-right.

title: Course Schedule Feasibility
description: Given numCourses and a list of prerequisite pairs [a, b] meaning you must take course b before course a, determine whether it is possible to finish all courses. Return true if a valid course order exists, false if a cycle exists.

examples.explanation: Take course 0 first, then course 1. No cycle exists.

title: Shortest Path in Weighted Graph
description: Given n nodes numbered 0 to n-1, a list of weighted directed edges [u, v, w], and a source node src, return an array where dist[i] is the shortest distance from src to node i. If a node is unreachable, set its distance to -1. Use Dijkstra's algorithm.

examples.explanation: Shortest distances from node 0: to 0=0, to 1=2, to 2=3, to 3=9, to 4=6.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing a staircase with n steps. Each time you can climb either 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top? Return the number of distinct ways.

title: 0/1 Knapsack Problem
description: Given n items each with a weight and value, and a knapsack with capacity W, determine the maximum total value you can put in the knapsack. Each item can either be taken or not (0/1). You cannot take fractional amounts.

examples.explanation: Taking items with weights 3 and 4 gives values 4+5 = 9, which is the maximum.

title: Edit Distance Between Two Strings
description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace a character) required to convert word1 into word2. This is the classic Levenshtein distance problem.

examples.explanation: horse -> rorse (replace h with r) -> rose (remove r) -> ros (remove e). 3 operations.

title: Assign Cookies to Children
description: Each child i has a greed factor g[i] and each cookie j has a size s[j]. A child is content if s[j] >= g[i]. Assign cookies to maximize the number of content children. Each child can receive at most one cookie.

examples.explanation: Only child with greed 1 can be satisfied. Maximum content children is 1.

title: Jump Game Reachability
description: Given an integer array nums where nums[i] represents the maximum jump length from index i, determine if you can reach the last index starting from index 0. Return true if you can reach the end, false otherwise.

examples.explanation: Jump 1 to index 1, then 3 steps to the last index.

title: Minimum Number of Platforms
description: Given arrival and departure times of trains at a station, find the minimum number of platforms required so no train has to wait. Each train's arrival and departure are given as integers (e.g., 900 = 9:00 AM). A train occupies a platform from arrival to departure inclusive.

examples.explanation: At peak time (950-1120), three trains are present simultaneously.

title: Generate All Subsets
description: Given an integer array nums with distinct elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the subsets in any order.

examples.explanation: All 2^3 = 8 subsets of [1,2,3].

title: Permutations of Unique Elements
description: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all possible permutations in any order. Each permutation must use all elements exactly once.

examples.explanation: There are 3! = 6 permutations of [1,2,3].

title: Sudoku Solver
description: Given a partially filled 9x9 Sudoku board, fill in the board to complete the puzzle. Each row, column, and 3x3 sub-box must contain all digits from 1 to 9 exactly once. The given board has a unique solution. Modify the board in-place.

examples.explanation: The board is filled such that each row, column, and 3x3 box contains digits 1-9 with no repetition.

title: Square Root via Binary Search
description: Given a non-negative integer x, compute and return the integer square root of x. The integer square root is the greatest integer whose square is less than or equal to x. Do not use any built-in exponent or square root functions.

examples.explanation: sqrt(8) ≈ 2.828, and the integer part is 2.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Given a rotated sorted array nums with unique integers and a target, return the index of target if it exists, otherwise return -1. The array was sorted in ascending order and then rotated at some unknown pivot. Your solution must run in O(log n) time.

examples.explanation: Target 0 is at index 4 in the rotated array.

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
description: Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2 of sizes m and n, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity must be O(log(m+n)).

examples.explanation: Merged array is [1,2,3] and the median is 2.0.

title: Find K Closest Elements
description: Given a sorted integer array arr, two integers k and x, return the k closest integers to x in sorted order. Distance is defined as |a - x|. Ties are broken by choosing the smaller element.

examples.explanation: The 4 closest elements to 3 are 1, 2, 3, and 4.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequently occurring elements. You may return the answer in any order. The answer is guaranteed to be unique.

examples.explanation: 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 2 times. The top 2 frequent elements are 1 and 2.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description: Design a data structure that supports adding integers from a data stream and finding the median at any time. Implement addNum(int num) to add a number and findMedian() to return the current median. If even count, return average of two middle values.

examples.explanation: After [1,2], median is 1.5. After [1,2,3], median is 2.0.

title: Count Set Bits
description: Given a non-negative integer n, return the number of 1-bits in its binary representation (also known as the Hamming weight or popcount). Do not use any string conversion functions.

examples.explanation: 11 in binary is 1011, which has three 1-bits.

title: Single Number in Array
description: Given an integer array where every element appears exactly three times except for one element which appears exactly once, find that single element. Your solution must run in O(n) time and O(1) space. Do not sort the array.

examples.explanation: 2 appears three times and 3 appears once, so 3 is the answer.

title: Reverse Bits of an Integer
description: Reverse the bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer and return the result. For example, if the input binary representation is 00000010100101000001111010011100, the reversed binary is 00111001011110000010100101000000.
